title: Alphabet Just Joined the Dow Jones Industrial Average. 3 Dow Dividend Stocks to Buy Now.
author: Patrick Sanders
description: Alphabet joined the Dow Jones Industrial Average on June 29, replacing Verizon. While Alphabet is a strong company with growing cloud and AI segments, its low dividend yield (0.3%) makes it less suitable for income investors. The article recommends three alternative Dow dividend stocks: Cisco Systems, Coca-Cola, and Amgen, all offering stronger dividend yields and solid year-to-date performance.

insights.sentiment_reasoning: Strong advertising business, fast-growing cloud segment with 63% revenue growth, and significant AI infrastructure investments. However, limited by low dividend yield (0.3%).
Strong advertising business, fast-growing cloud segment with 63% revenue growth, and significant AI infrastructure investments. However, limited by low dividend yield (0.3%).
Strong advertising business, fast-growing cloud segment with 63% revenue growth, and significant AI infrastructure investments. However, limited by low dividend yield (0.3%).
Strong advertising business, fast-growing cloud segment with 63% revenue growth, and significant AI infrastructure investments. However, limited by low dividend yield (0.3%).

Stock up 47% year-to-date, strong Q3 earnings with 12% revenue growth and 35% net income growth. AI products driving renewed investor attention. Dividend yield of 1.5% with 14 consecutive years of increases.
Stock up 18.2% year-to-date, Q1 revenue up 12% with organic growth of 10%. Strong dividend yield of 2.6% with 65 consecutive years of dividend increases (Dividend King status).

Stock up nearly 10% year-to-date, Q1 revenue up 6% with 16 products posting double-digit sales growth. Anti-obesity drug MariTide shows promise. Dividend yield of 2.8% with 15 consecutive years of increases.
Replaced by Alphabet in the Dow Jones Industrial Average. No specific performance metrics provided in the article.

title: 2 Warren Buffett Stocks to Buy and Hold for the Next 20 Years
author: Rachel Warren
description: The article recommends Coca-Cola and American Express as two long-term buy-and-hold stocks suitable for a 20-year investment horizon. Coca-Cola is praised for its economic moat, pricing power, and 64-year dividend streak, while American Express is highlighted for its unique closed-loop payment ecosystem, affluent customer base, and strong revenue growth. Both stocks are core holdings in Berkshire Hathaway's portfolio and are positioned to benefit from inflation through their pricing power and fee structures.

insights.sentiment_reasoning: Strong economic moat, 64-year dividend increase streak, global brand recognition, pricing power to pass costs to consumers, and resilience across economic cycles make it an attractive long-term holding.

Unique closed-loop payment ecosystem, affluent customer base with low default risk, record $72 billion revenue with 10% growth, 15% earnings per share surge, built-in inflation hedge through percentage-based fees, and consistent dividend increases support long-term growth potential.

Mentioned as the long-term holder of both stocks since 1988, demonstrating confidence in these investments, but the article focuses on the individual stocks rather than Berkshire itself as an investment recommendation.

Mentioned as the long-term holder of both stocks since 1988, demonstrating confidence in these investments, but the article focuses on the individual stocks rather than Berkshire itself as an investment recommendation.

title: Warren Buffett's Favorite Holdings: 3 Stocks Worth Owning for a Lifetime
author: Adria Cimino
description: Warren Buffett's three largest holdings in Berkshire Hathaway—Coca-Cola, American Express, and Apple—are highlighted as long-term investments worthy of lifetime ownership. Coca-Cola benefits from strong brand moat and 50+ years of dividend increases. American Express demonstrates resilience and growing younger customer base. Apple, despite being a tech exception for Buffett, offers strong competitive advantages and recurring services revenue.

insights.sentiment_reasoning: Praised for solid competitive moat, brand strength, deep distribution network, and status as Dividend King with 50+ consecutive years of dividend increases. Buffett has held shares for decades with intention to hold 'forever.'

Highlighted as second-largest position in Berkshire portfolio, demonstrating strength in economic downturns, growing younger customer base (66% of new accounts from Millennials/Gen Z), and strong free cash flow supporting dividend sustainability.

Positioned as largest holding in Buffett's portfolio with strong competitive moat in smartphones and devices. Praised for well-run business, leadership under Tim Cook, large installed base of 2.5+ billion devices, and explosive growth in recurring services revenue.
Buffett's holding company has delivered compounded annual gains of nearly 20% versus S&P 500's 10%, demonstrating long-term investing success and market-beating performance over six decades.
Buffett's holding company has delivered compounded annual gains of nearly 20% versus S&P 500's 10%, demonstrating long-term investing success and market-beating performance over six decades.

title: Move Over, Coca-Cola: Warren Buffett's Successor, Greg Abel, Has a New No. 3 Holding at Berkshire Hathaway
author: Sean Williams
description: Greg Abel, Warren Buffett's successor as CEO of Berkshire Hathaway, has significantly increased the company's stake in Alphabet through a $10 billion private placement, elevating it to the No. 3 position in Berkshire's portfolio ahead of long-held Coca-Cola. The move signals Abel's confidence in Alphabet's AI leadership and cloud growth potential, marking a shift toward tech investments that Buffett historically avoided.

insights.sentiment_reasoning: Berkshire tripled its Class A stake and opened a new Class C position, plus committed $10 billion in a private placement. Alphabet is positioned as an AI leader with strong Google Cloud growth (63% acceleration) and a sustainable competitive moat in search with ~90% market share.

Berkshire tripled its Class A stake and opened a new Class C position, plus committed $10 billion in a private placement. Alphabet is positioned as an AI leader with strong Google Cloud growth (63% acceleration) and a sustainable competitive moat in search with ~90% market share.

Berkshire tripled its Class A stake and opened a new Class C position, plus committed $10 billion in a private placement. Alphabet is positioned as an AI leader with strong Google Cloud growth (63% acceleration) and a sustainable competitive moat in search with ~90% market share.

Berkshire tripled its Class A stake and opened a new Class C position, plus committed $10 billion in a private placement. Alphabet is positioned as an AI leader with strong Google Cloud growth (63% acceleration) and a sustainable competitive moat in search with ~90% market share.

While displaced as No. 3 holding, Coca-Cola remains a core long-term position since 1988 with exceptional dividend yield (65% on cost) and unrivaled geographic diversity. The article emphasizes it 'isn't going anywhere,' indicating continued confidence despite the portfolio shift.

Remains Berkshire's largest holding by market value, but the article notes the investment team hasn't shown 'such decisive buying' in Apple as they have in Alphabet, suggesting a shift in investment focus rather than concern about Apple.

Under new leadership, Berkshire is actively repositioning its portfolio toward high-growth AI opportunities, demonstrating strategic agility and confidence in emerging technology trends while maintaining core dividend-paying positions.

title: The AI Trade Is Wobbling. Berkshire Hathaway Was Built for a Market Like This.
author: James Brumley
description: AI stocks like Alphabet, Amazon, Microsoft, and Meta are experiencing significant weakness amid concerns about AI spending and executive departures. The article suggests Berkshire Hathaway is well-positioned to weather potential market downturns due to its defensive holdings, $339.3 billion in Treasury bills yielding ~4%, and privately-owned cash-generating businesses that reduce volatility.

insights.sentiment_reasoning: Stock down 15% from mid-May peak; lost key AI executives to competitors OpenAI and Anthropic
